agent_output: {"executive_summary":"The courier assignment system faces a direct CAP theorem conflict: requirements 2 (strong consistency — no double-assignment) and 3 (high availability during node failure) are fundamentally at odds. This is not a design problem to solve — it is a physics constraint to acknowledge and navigate. The recommended architecture prioritizes consistency over availability (CP), uses a single-leader PostgreSQL with synchronous replication and optimistic locking for assignment, and achieves the 200ms latency target through in-memory pre-filtering of available couriers. For a 2-engineer team, simplicity is the dominant architectural constraint — we avoid distributed consensus, event sourcing, and microservices in favor of a well-structured monolith.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway / Load Balancer","responsibility":"Route incoming order placement requests. Health-check the primary database to detect failover.","boundaries":"Stateless. No business logic. Only routing and TLS termination."},{"name":"Assignment Service (Monolith)","responsibility":"Core business logic: receive order, select best available courier, atomically assign. Encapsulates the assignment algorithm, courier availability tracking, and order lifecycle.","boundaries":"Single deployable unit. Owns all domain logic. Communicates only with PostgreSQL and the courier location cache."},{"name":"PostgreSQL (Primary + Sync Replica)","responsibility":"Source of truth for courier assignments, order state, and courier availability. Synchronous replication ensures the replica has committed data before acknowledging writes.","boundaries":"Single-leader writes. Replica serves read queries and is promotion-ready. No multi-master."},{"name":"Courier Availability Cache (In-Process)","responsibility":"Maintains a hot set of available couriers with last-known location, refreshed every few seconds from DB + courier heartbeats. Enables sub-200ms candidate selection without hitting the DB for reads.","boundaries":"Eventually consistent read model. Never used for assignment decisions — only for pre-filtering candidates. The DB is the authority."},{"name":"Courier Location Ingestion","responsibility":"Receives GPS pings from courier apps via lightweight endpoint, updates location in DB and cache.","boundaries":"Write-only path. Tolerate dropped pings gracefully (couriers send frequently)."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Assignment Service","contract":"POST /orders — accepts order payload, returns assigned courier within 200ms or 503","coupling_type":"loose (HTTP, stateless)"},{"from":"Assignment Service","to":"PostgreSQL","contract":"BEGIN; SELECT courier WHERE available=true AND id=:candidate FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED; UPDATE courier SET available=false, current_order=:order_id; COMMIT;","coupling_type":"tight (transactional dependency, acceptable — this IS the consistency boundary)"},{"from":"Assignment Service","to":"Courier Availability Cache","contract":"In-process read of available courier set, sorted by proximity/score","coupling_type":"tight (in-process, by design for latency)"},{"from":"Courier App","to":"Courier Location Ingestion","contract":"POST /couriers/:id/location — lat/lng/timestamp, fire-and-forget","coupling_type":"loose (async, loss-tolerant)"}],"data_flow":"1. Order arrives at API Gateway → routed to Assignment Service. 2. Assignment Service reads Courier Availability Cache to get ranked candidate list (sub-1ms). 3. Assignment Service attempts atomic assignment in PostgreSQL using SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED on top candidate. 4. If candidate was already taken (race), automatically tries next candidate in ranked list (retry within same transaction). 5. On success, returns assigned courier. Cache is updated asynchronously. 6. If no courier available or DB unreachable, return 503 with retry-after header.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Optimistic Concurrency with SKIP LOCKED","rationale":"PostgreSQL's SKIP LOCKED allows concurrent assignment attempts to automatically skip already-locked couriers rather than blocking. This eliminates contention and guarantees no double-assignment while keeping latency low. Far simpler than distributed locks."},{"pattern":"CQRS-lite (Read Cache + Write DB)","rationale":"Separating the read path (in-memory cache for candidate selection) from the write path (PostgreSQL for atomic assignment) achieves the 200ms target without sacrificing consistency. The cache is never authoritative — it only narrows the candidate set."},{"pattern":"Single-Leader Replication with Synchronous Standby","rationale":"PostgreSQL synchronous replication ensures zero data loss on failover. Combined with automatic promotion (pg_auto_failover or Patroni), this gives the best consistency-availability trade-off for a small team."},{"pattern":"Monolithic Architecture","rationale":"Two engineers cannot operationally sustain microservices. A well-structured monolith with clear internal module boundaries gives all the benefits of separation of concerns without the deployment and debugging overhead."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Order"]},{"name":"Courier Assignment","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierAvailability","Assignment"]},{"name":"Courier Tracking","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CourierLocation"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier Assignment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Management publishes order-created events (in-process); Courier Assignment consumes them to trigger assignment logic."},{"upstream":"Courier Tracking","downstream":"Courier Assignment","relationship":"Conformist — Courier Assignment reads location data as-is from the tracking context's model. No translation needed."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Prioritize Consistency over Availability (CP system)","gains":"Zero double-assignments guaranteed. Simpler mental model — one source of truth. No conflict resolution logic needed.","sacrifices":"During a failover window (typically 5-30 seconds with Patroni), assignment requests will fail with 503. Orders placed during this window must be retried by the client.","alternatives_considered":["AP system with conflict resolution — allows assignments during partitions but requires complex last-writer-wins or merge logic to resolve double-assignments after the fact. Unacceptable given requirement 2.","Multi-master PostgreSQL (BDR) — provides availability during node failure but introduces conflict resolution complexity and risks double-assignment during split-brain. Violates requirement 2."]},{"decision":"In-process cache instead of Redis","gains":"Eliminates a network hop (~1-3ms saved). One fewer infrastructure component to operate. No Redis failure mode to handle.","sacrifices":"Cache is per-instance — multiple Assignment Service instances have slightly different views. Acceptable because cache is only for pre-filtering, not for decisions.","alternatives_considered":["Redis as shared cache — adds operational burden, network latency, and a new failure mode. Not justified for a 2-person team."]},{"decision":"Monolith over microservices","gains":"Single deployment pipeline. One repo. Shared-memory communication. Dramatically simpler debugging, testing, and operations for 2 engineers.","sacrifices":"Cannot independently scale or deploy the assignment logic separately from order management. Acceptable at this scale.","alternatives_considered":["Microservices with message broker — operationally unjustifiable for 2 engineers. Would spend more time on infrastructure than features."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Assignment Algorithm","evolution_path":"The courier ranking/scoring logic is behind an internal interface (CourierRanker). Can evolve from simple proximity-based to ML-scored without touching the assignment transaction logic."},{"area":"Cache Strategy","evolution_path":"Can migrate from in-process cache to Redis later if horizontal scaling demands a shared read model. The cache interface is the same either way."},{"area":"Event Publishing","evolution_path":"Assignment events are emitted in-process now but can be published to a message broker later for analytics, notifications, or new consumers without changing the core assignment logic."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"PostgreSQL as consistency boundary","reason":"The entire double-assignment guarantee depends on PostgreSQL transactions. Migrating to a different datastore would require re-implementing the consistency mechanism. This is intentional — the database IS the serialization point."},{"area":"Single-leader writes","reason":"All assignment writes go through one DB leader. This is the correct trade-off for consistency but means write throughput is bounded by a single node. At very high scale (thousands of assignments/second), this becomes a bottleneck requiring sharding by geography."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Failover window causes order assignment failures for 5-30 seconds","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use Patroni or pg_auto_failover for automated promotion. Client-side retry with exponential backoff. Queue orders in the API gateway during failover and replay. Monitor failover duration and alert if >10s."},{"risk":"200ms latency budget exceeded under load","severity":"medium","mitigation":"The SKIP LOCKED pattern avoids row-level contention. Pre-filter via cache keeps DB queries minimal. Load test with realistic concurrency. Add connection pooling (PgBouncer) to avoid connection overhead."},{"risk":"In-process cache becomes stale, leading to repeated failed assignment attempts","severity":"low","mitigation":"Cache refresh interval of 2-3 seconds. Even with stale data, the DB transaction is the authority — a stale cache just means trying a courier who is already taken, which SKIP LOCKED handles gracefully by moving to the next candidate."},{"risk":"Two engineers is a bus-factor risk for operational knowledge","severity":"high","mitigation":"Keep architecture deliberately simple (monolith, PostgreSQL, minimal moving parts). Document runbooks for failover scenarios. Use managed PostgreSQL (RDS/Cloud SQL) to offload operational burden."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Build the Assignment Service as a single Go or Node.js application with PostgreSQL. Implement the SKIP LOCKED assignment pattern first and load test it — this is the critical path. Phase 2: Add the in-process courier availability cache with periodic refresh. Phase 3: Set up PostgreSQL synchronous replication with Patroni for automated failover. Use a managed database service if possible to reduce operational load.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/assignment/courier_ranker.ts — Interface and implementation for ranking available couriers","src/assignment/assignment_service.ts — Core assignment logic with SKIP LOCKED transaction","src/assignment/availability_cache.ts — In-process cache of available couriers, refreshed periodically","src/orders/order_controller.ts — HTTP handler for order placement, delegates to assignment service","src/couriers/location_ingestion.ts — Endpoint for courier GPS updates","db/migrations/001_create_couriers.sql — couriers table with available flag, current_order, location","db/migrations/002_create_orders.sql — orders table with assigned_courier_id, status","db/migrations/003_create_assignments.sql — assignment audit log for traceability"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use the Repository pattern for database access. Keep the assignment transaction in a single method — do not spread it across service boundaries. The CourierRanker is a pure function (candidates in, ranked list out) with no side effects. Use dependency injection for testability — inject the ranker and repository into the assignment service.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["DO NOT use application-level distributed locks (Redis SETNX, etc.) — PostgreSQL row-level locking is simpler, faster, and transactionally safe","DO NOT make the cache authoritative for availability — it is a performance optimization only, the DB is always the source of truth","DO NOT retry assignment at the HTTP client level without idempotency keys — you risk double-order-creation","DO NOT use async/eventual consistency for the assignment write path — the double-assignment invariant requires synchronous transactional guarantees","DO NOT introduce Kafka, RabbitMQ, or any message broker in v1 — a 2-person team cannot afford the operational overhead"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_constraints","description":"Requirements 2 (strong consistency) and 3 (high availability) are in direct tension per the CAP theorem. The product team must explicitly acknowledge that during a database failover window (5-30 seconds), order assignments will fail and must be retried. If true zero-downtime is required even during node failures, requirement 2 (no double-assignment) cannot be absolutely guaranteed — the team must choose which invariant to relax.","options":["CP: Guarantee no double-assignment, accept brief unavailability during failover (recommended)","AP: Stay available during failures, accept risk of occasional double-assignment that must be resolved after the fact","Hybrid: Use CP for assignment but queue orders during failover for delayed processing (still unavailable in real-time but no lost orders)"],"recommendation":"CP with client-side retry and order queuing during failover. Brief unavailability (5-30s) during rare node failures is vastly preferable to the operational nightmare of resolving double-assignments in a logistics system."}],"confidence":{"level":55,"high_confidence_areas":["SKIP LOCKED pattern for contention-free atomic assignment","Monolith is correct for 2-engineer team","In-process cache + DB write separation achieves 200ms target","PostgreSQL synchronous replication for failover"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact failover duration depends on infrastructure choices (managed vs self-hosted, Patroni config)","Whether 200ms is achievable end-to-end depends on network topology and courier fleet size — needs load testing","Product team's actual tolerance for unavailability during failover is unstated"],"assumptions":["Courier fleet size is in the thousands, not millions (single PostgreSQL node can handle the write volume)","The 200ms requirement is p99, not p100","Product team will accept 503s during database failover with client retry","Managed PostgreSQL (RDS, Cloud SQL) is available to reduce operational burden on the small team"]}}
